steps.title: Angles in Standard Position
steps.goal: See how an angle is swept out from the positive x-axis.
steps.introText: An angle in **standard position** has its vertex at the origin and its initial side along the positive x-axis. Sweeping counterclockwise gives a positive angle; the ray you end on is the **terminal side**.
steps.prompt: Drag the orange point around the circle. Watch how the swept angle θ grows from 0 and how the same rotation can be measured in degrees or radians.

steps.title: Radians & Degrees
steps.goal: Connect common special angles in degrees to their radian measures.
steps.introText: One full turn is 360° or **2π radians**, so 180° = π. That makes the common special angles 30° = **π/6**, 45° = **π/4**, 60° = **π/3**, and 90° = **π/2**.
steps.prompt: Visit at least three different special angles. The point snaps to common angles so you can read each one in both degrees and radians.

steps.title: Sweep to a Right Angle
steps.goal: Place the terminal side at a quarter turn.
steps.prompt: Drag the terminal point to θ = π/2 (90°).

feedback.correct: Exactly! A quarter turn is π/2 radians, which equals 90°.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet — π/2 is a quarter of a full turn, straight up the positive y-axis.
feedback.hint: π/2 radians = 90°. Sweep counterclockwise until the point sits on the positive y-axis.

steps.title: Find the Quadrant
steps.goal: Identify where an angle's terminal side lands.
steps.info: The axes split the plane into four **quadrants**, numbered counterclockwise starting from the upper right (Quadrant I).
steps.prompt: Place the terminal point anywhere in Quadrant II (between 90° and 180°).

feedback.correct: Nice! Quadrant II holds angles between π/2 and π.
feedback.incorrect: Quadrant II is the upper-left region — angles between 90° and 180°.
feedback.hint: Sweep past 90° (π/2) but stop before 180° (π) to land in the upper-left quadrant.

steps.title: A Bigger Angle
steps.prompt: Drag the terminal point to θ = 5π/6 (150°).

feedback.correct: Great! 5π/6 is 150°, just 30° short of a half turn.
feedback.incorrect: Not quite — 5π/6 is 150°, in Quadrant II.
feedback.hint: 5π/6 = 150°. It is the reflection of π/6 (30°) across the y-axis.

steps.title: Check Your Understanding
steps.prompt: How many radians is a full turn (360°)?
steps.feedback: Correct! One full revolution is 2π radians, so 180° = π radians.
steps.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. A half turn (180°) is π radians, so a full turn is twice that.

steps.title: Mastery Check
steps.goal: Reach four target angles — no hints.
steps.completionMessage: Excellent! You can now measure angles in radians and locate them on the unit circle. Next, we'll read sine and cosine straight off the circle.
